skill.name: webapp-nikto
skill.description: Guides authorized Nikto web server vulnerability scans, including target setup, scan tuning, reporting formats, and remediation-focused review.
skill.summary: Run authorized Nikto web server vulnerability assessments with structured reporting guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, network, script, environment, and filesystem patterns. Most are false positives from documentation templates or expected Nikto usage, but the skill contains confirmed dual-use scanning guidance, IDS/WAF evasion examples, and a CI template that pipes a remote installer into a shell. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious payloads, so this is not blocked as malware.

high_findings.title: IDS and WAF Evasion Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill gives explicit Nikto evasion and WAF-blocking workarounds. Even with authorization warnings, this materially increases dual-use risk because it helps bypass detection during web scanning.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly labels an evasion section and shows commands for IDS evasion and WAF blocking scenarios. The authorization framing lowers malicious-intent confidence but not the dual-use risk.

high_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell in CI Template
high_findings.description: The CI template demonstrates downloading a remote shell installer and piping it directly to bash. This is a supply-chain risk if copied because remote content executes inside CI without pinning or verification.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is direct remote code execution in a CI job. It appears instructional rather than malicious, but it remains an unsafe pattern for marketplace users.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Web Vulnerability Scanning Can Affect Targets
medium_findings.description: The skill is built around running Nikto against web servers. It warns users to get written permission and notes traffic, alerting, log volume, and possible production impact.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The intended functionality is active web server scanning, which inherently sends network traffic to targets. The documented authorization checks show legitimate intent, so this is elevated dual-use risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Inline Credentials in Authenticated Scan Examples
medium_findings.description: Authenticated scanning examples place usernames, passwords, and cookies directly in command lines. These are placeholders, but copied patterns can leak credentials through shell history, logs, or process listings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clearly placeholders for Nikto usage, not credential theft. The risk is still credible because users may copy the command structure with real secrets.

medium_findings.title: Bulk Scanning Workflow Increases Blast Radius
medium_findings.description: The skill shows multi-host and bulk scan workflows. This is useful for authorized assessment, but it can create high traffic or scan unintended systems if scope controls are weak.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands directly support scanning multiple targets. Authorization language exists elsewhere, so the concern is operational risk and misuse potential rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Environment Token Access Is Limited to Security Tool Configuration
low_findings.description: The GitHub token and API key references are used in CI or secure-code examples. No evidence found that the skill exfiltrates secrets or instructs users to reveal tokens.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The static patterns are real environment access references, but semantic context shows secrets scanning and secure API examples. There is no network exfiltration path tied to these values.

low_findings.title: Vulnerable Code Snippets Are Pedagogical Examples
low_findings.description: Static hits for XSS, weak cryptography, hardcoded secrets, and injection occur inside rule templates or reference examples that show vulnerable and fixed patterns for education.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files label these blocks as examples and include remediation or fixed versions. The content can still be copied incorrectly, but it is not executable skill behavior.

low_findings.title: Local File Reads Are for Generated Report Comments
low_findings.description: The Node.js filesystem access in the CI template reads a locally generated markdown report before creating a pull request comment. No evidence found of arbitrary file reads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filename is hardcoded to the report generated earlier in the same job. The pattern is legitimate CI automation, although it remains a filesystem access risk factor.

dangerous_patterns.title: Nikto Evasion Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows use of Nikto evasion options and WAF bypass troubleshooting. This pattern can reduce defender visibility during scans.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct and explicitly described as evasion or WAF blocking solutions. Authorization language exists, but the pattern remains sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Script Execution in Pipeline
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI template pipes a downloaded installer directly to bash. This can execute compromised or changed upstream code in a trusted CI environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line uses a known unsafe install pattern. The context is a template, not a hidden payload, which limits confidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Inline Authentication Material in Shell Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The Nikto examples pass passwords and cookies directly through command arguments. This can expose secrets through logs, shell history, or process inspection.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The credentials are placeholders, but the command pattern is realistic. The risk depends on whether users substitute real secrets into copied commands.

content.user_title: Run Authorized Nikto Web Server Assessments
content.value_statement: Web server misconfigurations and outdated components are hard to review consistently. This skill guides authorized Nikto scans, result handling, and remediation-focused reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides single-host, multi-port, SSL, authenticated, and bulk Nikto scan workflows.
Explains Nikto tuning options, plugins, timeouts, redirects, and output formats.
Provides report generation patterns for text, HTML, CSV, XML, and CI artifacts.
Maps web server assessment activity to OWASP, CWE, NIST, PCI-DSS, and ISO 27001 concepts.
Includes operational safeguards for written authorization, scan windows, rate limiting, and audit logging.
Supplies templates for CI security scanning and security rule documentation.
limitations: Requires Nikto and Perl to be installed in the execution environment.
Does not verify legal authorization or ownership of target systems.
Nikto findings can include false positives that need manual validation.
Active scans can create traffic, logs, alerts, and service impact on production systems.

use_cases.title: External Web Server Review
use_cases.description: Plan and run authorized Nikto checks against approved internet-facing web servers, then prepare findings for remediation.

use_cases.title: Internal Hardening Validation
use_cases.description: Validate web server patching, configuration, headers, and exposed files before release or after infrastructure changes.

use_cases.title: Compliance Evidence Collection
use_cases.description: Generate structured scan artifacts and audit notes that support vulnerability management and web server hardening controls.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Basic Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one approved target
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Nikto scan for one web server I am authorized to test. Include scope, command options, and output format.

prompt_templates.title: Tune Scan Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused testing with specific vulnerability categories
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a Nikto scan plan for approved targets that focuses on misconfiguration, information disclosure, and outdated software checks.

prompt_templates.title: Create a CI Scan Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Automated assessment in a controlled pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a CI workflow that runs authorized Nikto scans, stores reports, and fails only on agreed severity criteria.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze and Prioritize Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Nikto findings, group likely false positives, map confirmed issues to OWASP and CWE, and recommend remediation priorities.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a scan plan for one authorized HTTPS web server.
output_examples.output: A short assessment checklist with authorization confirmation, target URL, scan window, safe timeout settings, report format, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A user provides Nikto results with several server configuration findings.
output: Likely false positives separated from confirmed issues.
Remediation guidance linked to web server hardening controls.

output_examples.input: A team wants scheduled web server scans in CI.
output_examples.output: A pipeline outline with controlled target scope, artifact retention, alerting rules, and manual review before enforcement.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, target scope, ports, and approved scan windows before running active scans.
Start with conservative timing and focused tuning, then expand coverage only when impact is acceptable.
Manually validate findings before reporting severity or assigning remediation work.
anti_patterns: Do not scan systems without explicit permission from the owner.
Do not put real passwords, tokens, or session cookies directly in shared command lines.
Do not treat Nikto output as final proof without context and manual verification.

faq.question: Does this skill run Nikto by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command patterns. The user or agent environment must have Nikto installed and authorized targets defined.

faq.question: Is this only for offensive security teams?
faq.answer: No. It is useful for defenders, consultants, and DevSecOps teams who perform authorized web server assessments.

faq.question: Can it scan production systems?
faq.answer: Only with explicit approval and an agreed scan window. Nikto can generate many requests and trigger monitoring systems.

faq.question: Does it replace manual vulnerability validation?
faq.answer: No. Nikto can produce false positives. Human review is needed before confirming risk or assigning remediation.

faq.question: Can it create compliance evidence?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help structure scan records, findings, remediation notes, and references for vulnerability management controls.

faq.question: What are the main safety concerns?
faq.answer: The main concerns are unauthorized scanning, excessive traffic, credential exposure in commands, and misuse of evasion options.
